show_notes:
Abram Chayes — The Role of the Judge in Public Law Litigation (89 Harv. L. Rev. 1281 (1976))

Abram Chayes argues that the traditional model of civil adjudication — a bipolar, retrospective, party-controlled dispute over private rights — no longer captures much of what federal trial courts actually do. In its place he identifies an emerging model of 'public law litigation': sprawling, many-party suits seeking prospective, legislative-like injunctive relief, in which the judge becomes an active organizer and ongoing administrator of remedies that reach far beyond the named parties. Chayes contends that this expanded judicial role, though it raises real concerns of legitimacy, is workable and perhaps inevitable in a heavily regulated society.
